text.content:
Phase 7 — Primary Nodes

Seven nodes. Any three identify the Cairn Hills region. Any four identify the Seven-Cut Cairn itself. No single node is required.

Node summary

#NodeLocationYields
1Iren VelashVelash Manuscripts & Bindings, Garden QuarterName, Function, partial Place
2Mistress Aldea VerenCottage in the wood (in person or by courier)Material, Function, partial Place
3Edrik Vone / Archive RemnantsHigh Quay, near Customs HousePermission, Place, Name
4Guild of Miners & Jewelers + Dalmor VetchGuild archive + a retired surveyor's housePlace, Function, Material
5Prisoner / WitnessWherever the party is holding Haskur, Kethren, Korre, or a turned K&R clerkVaries by NPC (see Node 5)
6The False StairAn abandoned culvert or dry quarry shaft outside GreyhawkReverse-evidence: rules out a false answer
7Field ReconnaissanceOld mining spur road northeast of Greyhawk CityMaterial, Place — and ends the phase at the Seven-Cut Cairn

Timing

Each visit to a primary node consumes half an in-game day. Field reconnaissance consumes one full day each way. The full phase is intended to play in 4–7 in-game days. The Route-Cleansing Clock advances daily; loud action also advances it.

Node 1 — Iren Velash (Ash and Glossary)

Velash Manuscripts & Bindings, Garden Quarter, Greyhawk City. Iren is the party's interpretive anchor for Phase 7. She is not friendly, not corrupt, and honest in the irritating way of exact people. She is willing to be useful because she is finally reading something that matters.

Read aloud — night or early morning

Iren has cleared the reading table. She has pinned down the Ashen Itinerary — or what survives of it — with four river-stones and a single thin band of black silk along the burnt edge so it does not crumble. The brass reading lamp is at low flame; the shutters are half-drawn. The shop smells of paper, of paste, and faintly of the iron-gall ink she uses to make notes.

She does not look up when you come in. “Sit,” she says. “Do not lean on the table. I have been reading since the third bell.”

After a long moment: “This is not a sea-route term. It is not a road term. It is a descent term.”

What Iren establishes

  • VII is not a sequence in a ledger. It is a route-class marker.
  • “Stair” is used in old route glossaries for artificial descents — cut steps, switchback shafts, hauling platforms.
  • “Seventh Question” (Caelith's identification phrase) and “Seventh Stair” may be related through an old Aerdy index tradition where descents were catalogued by step-count and surveyor's question-number.
  • “No sail. No road.” is literal or nearly literal. The route is not on water and not on a chartered surface road.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation (read the Itinerary)13The “VII” was added with a different ink pressure — later than the rest of the document. Possibly added by a different hand.
History15Old Aerdy mining charters used Roman numerals for sealed-route classifications. The numeral VII indicated permitted-access descents.
Arcana15The burned itinerary was heat-reactive but not standard magical dictation. Whoever wrote it knew arcane bookbinding but did not use it to encode the words — only to make them disappear when handled wrong.

What Iren can read further if given more

Bring her the wax flake from Korre's apartment, Aldea's reply, the route mark sketch, Vone's irregular ledger excerpt, or any captured documents. Iren produces a single one-page combined reading per item, which deepens her conclusions:

  • + wax flake → she confirms the wax has a mineral-darkening agent associated with sealed, cool storage. Suggests a route used by people who do not see daylight often.
  • + Aldea's reply → the materials together cannot come from a sailed port. “Whoever wrote this did not pass through a harbor in living memory.”
  • + Vone's ledger → she recognises the obsolete account-class prefix VII from a glossary on her highest shelf. This is the keystone.
  • + route mark sketch → she takes a long time, then says, “The cuts are seven. The hill is not a hill. It is a shape an old surveyor used for a sealed entrance.”

Failure cases

If the party brings nothing or only one item, Iren still establishes Name and Function. She cannot give Place without more material to cross-reference. The phase does not dead-end — she will point them toward Aldea and the Guild.

Iren's price

The same as before: bring her copies of what you find. She is now asking for the chase. “If you find another piece, bring it to me before you bring it to anyone else.”

Evidence categories delivered

Name (always), Function (always), partial Place (if multiple items provided). Iren cannot directly supply Permission or full Place.

Node 2 — Mistress Aldea Veren (Material Memory)

Trina's old correspondent in the wood. Scholar, archivist, druidic intelligence contact — the person who reads matter the way Iren reads paper. Depending on the Phase 5/6 chain, she may be at her cottage, in temporary refuge, or only reachable by dead-drop courier.

Option A — visit in person (Aldea present)

Read aloud

The cottage is quiet under a thin grey sky. Smoke is rising from the chimney; the workshop window glows soft. The brindle dog at the door knows you now and only looks up.

Aldea opens the door before you knock. “You have brought it,” she says, and steps aside.

She has cleared her work-table. On the boards she has set: a small glass dish, two clean reading-glasses, a fine pair of brass tongs, three white candles, a sheet of black silk, and a single iron-gall pen. She is waiting for materials, not words.

What Aldea wants from the party

The wax, the stone dust, the route mark rubbing, the burned paper fragment, any ink or seal residue. She does not want speculation. She wants matter.

Option B — courier reply (Aldea not present)

The party leaves materials at the dead-drop (an old beech stump near the stream). Within three days, a folded packet arrives by the same channel containing Handout 2 — Aldea Veren's Material Reply.

What Aldea reads from the materials

The wax flake

  • Recipe-family unfamiliar. The closest match in her records is a fragment of old mine-seal wax used in some Cairn Hills sealed claims — a wax meant to survive damp and mineral seepage.
  • Contains a darkening agent associated with cool, lightless storage.
  • One non-standard resin component. She has seen the resin once before, on a 200-year-old document from a sealed survey.

The stone dust

  • Not river-stone. Not quarry rubble. Worked stone. Old. Iron-grey with a thread of green.
  • The green thread is a mineral consistent with certain inland deposits. She names two: the southern Cairn Hills, and the Stark Mounds. The Cairn Hills are the closer match by trace iron content.

The route mark (if rubbed or sketched)

  • The cut technique is old. Not Yeomanry, not modern Aerdy, not Suel.
  • The “hill” shape is convention, not topography — an old surveyor's mark for a sealed entrance.

Burned paper fragment

  • The paper has been treated with a fixative used in old mining-charter copying. It is not commercial stationery.

What Aldea will not say

She will not name the deeper faction, the species of the writer, the destination, or any organisation. When pressed:

“You are looking for a road because the paper taught you to. Stop obeying the paper.”

Aldea's optional complication

If Lie 2 (party pursuing wood correspondent) was active in Phase 6, the conspiracy has copied her packet without her knowing. Aldea will discover this by the third day: a small ink-mark on the inside seam of one envelope, which she did not make. She tells the party. They can:

  • Replace her dead-drop location.
  • Set a counter-watch.
  • Move Aldea to a safer location (Caelith can arrange this, if Closure didn't pass).

If the party does nothing, Route-Cleansing Clock +1 at the start of the next in-game day.

Evidence categories delivered

Material (always), Function (always), partial Place (Cairn Hills region narrowed).

pages.name: Node 3 — Edrik Vone or Archive Remnants

text.content:
Node 3 — Edrik Vone or Archive Remnants

Vone's private courier archive, High Quay. The party visited him in Phase 6; in Phase 7 he is either more expensive, frightened, or vanished. The archive's value has gone up; the route language is becoming a target for active erasure.

Option A — Vone survived Phase 6 and is still working

Vone meets the party in his reception room. The single sheet of paper on his desk is now angled, not perpendicular — a subtle tell that he is not as calm as he wishes to appear. Insight DC 13 spots this.

Read aloud

Vone is at his desk. The paper is not square. The reading-lamp is lit despite the morning sun through the side window — the lamp is closer to where he can knock it over, if he needs to.

“You will pay more this week,” he says. “The records are more dangerous to keep. A courier ledger says where things went. A route-exception says where the clerk was told not to ask. I have been keeping the wrong copies.”

Access costs (Phase 7 markup)

DocumentPrice
Regular ledger75 gp
Irregular ledger300 gp
Route-exception copies (NEW; Phase 7 only)500 gp or a credible promise of protective custody.

Modifiers

  • Lie 1 (Haskur in Rel Astra) was active in Phase 6: prices −30%.
  • Path C (kinetic): prices +50%, refusal of Yeomanry papers.
  • Korre captured: Vone takes Korre's apartment ledger off his price; he can no longer be implicated for it.

Option B — Vone has vanished

His reception is locked. The dressmaker on the ground floor says she has not seen him in three days. The party can:

  • Break into the second floor (DC 15 main door / DC 13 side service door) — the irregular archive is intact, but Vone took the route-exception copies with him.
  • Find his terrified assistant, Pesh, hiding at a Garden Quarter cousin's house. Pesh can give a partial account of the route-exception copies for 50 gp and a promise of protection (DC 14 Persuasion).
  • Trace Vone himself — he has gone to ground in the Free City of Greyhawk's other archive market, the unsanctioned cellar on Bracken Lane. Locating him takes a day.

What the records show

  • The phrase “Stair exception” appears only in old movement gaps — ledger periods where official courier accounts go quiet for 24–48 hours.
  • The Roman numeral VII is tied to obsolete Cairn Hills mining claims, not to any active courier route.
  • A relay-point name appears in one altered ledger line: Tarnsmere Toll. Vone does not know where it is; he never saw a packet leave the city under that name. He saw the name on a forwarding chit.
  • One recent packet bypassed normal Greyhawk delivery and moved northeast under a mining account code, not a merchant code.

Vone's useful line

“A courier ledger says where things went. A route-exception says where the clerk was told not to ask. You want the second sort. You will not find them filed where they belong.”

Evidence categories delivered

Permission (always, via route-exception language), Place (always, via Tarnsmere Toll and northeast mining-account routing), Name (always, via the VII account-class prefix).

pages.name: Node 4 — Guild of Miners & Jewelers + Dalmor Vetch

text.content:
Node 4 — Guild of Miners & Jewelers + Dalmor Vetch

The Greyhawk Guild of Miners and Jewelers is the civic-commercial institution that holds old claims, surveys, and arguments fossilised in ink. It is not conspiracy-controlled. Someone has been recently requesting or removing certain old records, and the senior clerks have not yet noticed which clients are doing this.

The Guild Archive

A respectable building on Bargemen's Street, with a public hall, a clerk's counter, a claims wall, and a back archive that requires either a member's escort or a small private payment to access.

Approach options

ApproachDCYields
Social (Persuasion + a real Yeomanry / Aerdy commercial reason)14Public hall access; one hour with a senior clerk; can request one old claim file.
Bribery (15–30 gp to the right clerk)n/aQuiet back-archive access for half a day. Risk: the clerk remembers the party.
Stealth (after hours)16Full archive access. Risk: triggers the Heat Clock if caught.
Scholar (Investigation + History)15Identifies the obsolete VII account class in the claim-class index and the missing Tarnsmere Spur file before raising it with anyone.
Legal (writ from a Greyhawk advocate or Caelith's seal)n/aFull access in 1 day with formal records. Triggers Heat Clock by 1.

What the records show

  • An old mining spur road northeast of Greyhawk City. The road appears on the Guild's old map but is missing from the current map.
  • A ruined tollhouse named Tarnsmere Toll appears in one claim ledger from 487 CY. It is described as “serving the Tarnsmere Spur claim.”
  • A cairn marker with seven descending cuts appears in a surveyor's note — one of those marginal scribbles that surveyors leave when something doesn't quite fit the survey.
  • A sealed survey station near the cairn appears on the old map but has been removed from the current.
  • One claim name is crossed out and rewritten as “unprofitable.” This phrase, in Guild bureaucratic code, usually means “someone wanted people to stop looking.”
  • A senior clerk's underclerk — named Yorth Pell (no relation to the Loftwick Pell) — has been making copies of these documents for an unfamiliar client over the past three weeks. He will admit this only under hard pressure (Persuasion 16 or Intimidation 18). He does not know what the client looks like; the requests came by note, with payment by the kind of merchant chit Vone could trace.

The retired surveyor — Dalmor Vetch

Vetch is the Guild's living memory of stairs. Elderly, blunt, missing two fingers from a winch accident in 552 CY. He hates clean maps. He lives in a small terrace house in Greyhawk's Old Walls quarter and is reachable through the Guild for a small fee or directly through the Hand of Coals barkeep (Phase 6 Tellan Verth's tavern), who is Vetch's cousin's brother-in-law.

Read aloud — at Vetch's kitchen table

Dalmor Vetch sets two heavy mugs of small beer on the table without asking. He has been waiting; his cousin's brother-in-law sent word an hour ago. He sits, drinks, and looks at each of you in turn over the rim of his mug.

“You're after stairs,” he says. “Not roads. Roads, you'd ask the Guild. You came to me.”

He taps the table with his maimed hand. “A road is what merchants call it when they can charge toll. Miners call it a stair when the earth charges first. Tell me what you have. Don't waste my afternoon.”

What Vetch knows

  • Old mining stairs in the Cairn Hills — switchback shafts, cut step descents, hauling platforms. He has surveyed three of them in his career. None of them were profitable. That was the point: they were the ones that opened onto something deeper that the Guild stopped letting people document.
  • The Tarnsmere Spur — he knows it by name. It is a mining spur road northeast of the city, off the old eastern trade route, ending at a ruined tollhouse. He visited it twice in his youth. The second visit, his surveyor's chain came up short at the cairn — not because the ground was uneven, but because the cairn's symbol was warm to the touch.
  • The seven cuts under the hill mark: he says quietly, after a long pause, “That is not a route mark. That is a permission mark. We learned not to count past six.”
  • He will draw the party a sketched map (Handout 5) for 20 gp and a promise that they do not say his name to the Guild.

Vetch's useful line

“Roads end. Stairs continue. The seventh cut tells you which kind you've found.”

Evidence categories delivered

Place (Tarnsmere Spur, ruined tollhouse, Seven-Cut Cairn), Function (mining stair vs road), Material (Vetch confirms the cairn's symbol is unusual in ways consistent with Aldea's wax findings).

pages.name: Node 5 — Prisoner Interrogation or Witness Bargain

text.content:
Node 5 — Prisoner Interrogation or Witness Bargain

Use this node only if Haskur, Kethren, Korre, or another key figure is captured or willing to bargain. The node provides direct but limited confirmation. No single captured NPC solves the phase — that would defeat the triangulation system — but each delivers different fragments.

Three tiers of reveal

Each captured NPC gives different types of evidence, in tiers:

NPCCategories deliveredBest approach
Haskur VandrellName, PermissionPersuasion + survival offer; he is bargaining for his life
Kethren IlvathFunction, Permission, partial PlaceNarrow literal questions; he will not monologue
V. KorrePlace, Permission, NameLegal protection or managed disappearance; she is negotiating
Captured K&R staff (Doras)Permission, partial PlaceWitness protection; he has a family

Haskur Vandrell

See Phase 7 — Opening A. Useful lines repeated here:

  • “The Stair is where I was to be handed off.”
  • “Korre knew the paper. The boy knew the descent.”
  • “The route was older than Sereth's people.”

Kethren Ilvath

See Phase 7 — Opening E. Useful lines repeated here:

  • “It is a permitted descent.”
  • “No one finds it. One is admitted.”
  • “Surface roads end at the marker.”
  • “The marker is seven cuts under a black hill.”

Kethren will not name any deeper faction. The party can ask narrow literal questions: “Northeast of where?” / “How far?” / “A day's ride?” He answers each one technically. He does not volunteer geography. Insight DC 16 reveals he is more afraid of recall than of any other outcome.

V. Korre

See Phase 7 — Opening F. Useful lines repeated here:

  • “Seven-Cut was a route account, not a place to me.”
  • “Tarnsmere Toll appears in the older ledger.”
  • “The packet was to be carried beyond my jurisdiction.”
  • “My jurisdiction ended at the marker.”

Captured Kestrel & Reed staff (Doras Kemmel)

If Doras was turned in Phase 6, he is now a sworn witness. He testifies that:

  • The packets to Korre's account moved through a mining account, not a merchant account.
  • The forwarding chits read “TS · Cairn” in a hand he did not recognise — not Aerdy commercial, not standard Greyhawk shorthand.
  • The most recent chit was filed under “NE Spur — route exception.”

Anti-omniscience rule

If the party tries to get the whole phase from one captured NPC, the NPC's answers degrade into “I don't know” or evasions. Each NPC has been told only what they need to know. The conspiracy is compartmentalised by design. The party must cross-reference. That is the phase.

Node 6 — The False Stair (Enemy Misdirection)

This node is an enemy countermeasure, not a required investigation step. Use it if the Route-Cleansing Clock reaches 3 or higher, or if the party has been threatening prisoners without offering survival, or if Opening G (everyone escaped) is in play.

The setup

The party receives or discovers a false map. The map is plausible — not obviously a forgery, not absurdly easy. The route on it leads to an abandoned culvert, dry quarry shaft, or false waymark outside Greyhawk City but in the wrong direction (west, not northeast; or north along the wrong spur).

How the party gets the false map

  • A “helpful” stranger at a tavern who claims to be a retired miner.
  • A planted document in Vone's archive (if Vone has been threatened or compromised).
  • A coerced statement from Yorth Pell (the Guild underclerk).
  • If Haskur is captured and threatened too hard: he gives them this map deliberately.

Clues that the map is false

Tell the players these are noticeable. Reward observation:

CheckDCReveals
Investigation (compare to Vetch's sketch)12Cuts on the marker are six, not seven.
Survival13The stone is recently chipped — weeks, not centuries.
Investigation (wax residue)14Wax planted at the site does not match Aldea's recipe-family.
Perception (field reconnaissance)13No old hauling scars on the cliff face. No surveyor's anchor holes.
Arcana / History (mineral analysis)14No mineral residue matching Aldea's findings.

If the party walks in unaware

A small ambush awaits. The enemy is not trying to kill them; they are trying to waste the party's day and confirm whether the party still has a real lead.

Encounter

  • 2 route-cleanser agents — Spy / Assassin-lite analogs, CR 1–2 each.
  • 1 hired Greyhawk tough — Thug or Veteran, CR 1.
  • Optional alchemical smoke or collapsing timber trap — DC 14 Dex save or 4d6 bludgeoning damage and prone.

Enemy goal

Disengage if any PC is captured, killed, or the smoke / timber trap fires. They do not fight to the death. They flee toward the nearest road and disperse.

The recovery clue

Even total tactical failure at the False Stair leaves the party with one usable piece of evidence: the false map itself, with its six cuts and its un-aged stone. Comparison with Aldea or Vetch produces the realisation that the true site has seven cuts — and that the enemy knows this and is trying to hide it. This functionally adds 1 evidence category (partial Place by exclusion) and confirms the party is on the right phrase.

Tone note

Do not make the False Stair a punishment. Make it a bruise with a receipt. The party should leave it angrier, not stupider.

Evidence categories delivered

Reverse-evidence — the party learns that “six cuts” is the planted answer, and by exclusion, the real marker has seven cuts. Counts as a partial Place for triangulation.

pages.name: Node 7 — Field Reconnaissance toward the Cairn Hills

text.content:
Node 7 — Field Reconnaissance toward the Cairn Hills

The party leaves Greyhawk City northeast on the old eastern trade route, picks up the abandoned mining spur, and follows it toward the southern Cairn Hills. This node moves the party from paper to place. It ends at the Seven-Cut Cairn.

Travel texture (not a slog)

The journey is two days each way. Spend two scenes' worth of game time on the outbound. The inbound is usually omitted — Phase 7 ends at the cairn.

The first day

The road north and east of Greyhawk City. Familiar countryside: farms, wagon ruts, a roadside inn called the Three Wickets where the party can stop for the night without fuss. By the second bell after midday they leave the eastern trade route and turn onto the abandoned mining spur. The road's character changes immediately: the surface is older, the ruts are deeper, and there is no inn for the rest of the journey.

The second day

The mining spur curves north into the lower folds of the Cairn Hills. Charcoal-burner smoke rises in the distance. The grass between the ruts is waist-high; horses pick their way carefully. By midday, the party should make Perception (DC 12) and Survival (DC 13) checks at intervals.

Three field clues (give all three over the second day)

  1. Old cart ruts that vanish into grass. A working road's ruts continue and join other roads. These ruts simply stop, at a point where the grass has grown over a stone marker that has been pushed flat.
  2. A cut stone waymark with the seventh mark scraped almost flat. Six cuts are sharp; the seventh has been worn down within the last week. The party is being followed by the route-cleansers, or they have been preceded by them.
  3. A dead or abandoned pack animal with no ordinary road tack. Its harness is wrong for a merchant route. The harness is set up for carrying through narrow places — the kind of fittings used to lead a pack mule down a shaft.

Optional encounter — choose one

A. Frightened local charcoal burners

Three burners and a dog. They have seen “strangers with too-clean boots” on the road in the last week. They will not testify in a city court, but they will draw the party a rough sketch of where they saw the strangers turn off the road.

B. A mining claimant who denies the spur exists

An old man riding south to Greyhawk. When asked about the Tarnsmere Spur he says, “That road has not been a road since I was a boy. Whatever you are looking for, you will not find on it.” Insight DC 13 reveals he is afraid, not dismissive. Insight DC 15 reveals he is being paid.

C. A disguised route-cleanser removing waymarks

A figure in a working-mason's apron with a small mallet, scoring a stone marker until the seventh cut is gone. He claims to be doing maintenance. If pressed, he runs; if cornered, he yields and admits to being paid to deface markers along the spur. He doesn't know who pays.

D. A silent watcher at dusk

A figure on a low ridge in the distance at evening, watching the party. When they look back at first light, the figure is gone. This is Kethren if he escaped Phase 6; otherwise a route-cleanser proxy. The encounter does not have to resolve — its job is to be felt.

Arrival at the Seven-Cut Cairn

By the evening of the second day, the party reaches the ruined tollhouse and the cairn. See: Phase 7 — The Seven-Cut Cairn. Phase 7 ends with discovery; Phase 8 begins with descent.

Evidence categories delivered

Material (stone, ruts, animal harness), Place (the cairn itself). Field reconnaissance is the only node that can complete the triangulation by itself if the party already has 3+ categories from documentary sources.
